skill.name: refactor-code
skill.description: Guides safe code refactoring with DRY cleanup, complexity reduction, performance checks, and regression prevention.
skill.summary: Refactor code in small, tested steps while protecting existing behavior.
skill.author: Crearize

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious intent or prompt injection. Most detections are Markdown code fences, documentation examples, Japanese text, or localhost documentation; the remaining real risk is that the skill instructs agents to edit files and run repository commands during refactoring.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to run git, build, test, and dev-server commands. This is legitimate for refactoring, but project scripts may execute arbitrary code in an untrusted repository, so execution should stay sandboxed and visible to the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and tied to normal refactoring verification. I found no evidence of credential access, exfiltration, destructive shell pipelines, or hidden command construction.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Modification Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill requires reading and editing code files, removing unused code, and updating tests. This is expected behavior, but it can affect source integrity if applied without review or version control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-editing intent is directly documented and necessary for the skill. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the instructions emphasize tests, review, and rollback.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections are Markdown fenced blocks and examples, not executable Ruby code. Some fenced blocks contain normal shell commands, which are evaluated separately as command guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is in SKILL.md documentation, and there is no Ruby source file or runtime wrapper. Line context shows code fences for bash, Java, TypeScript, Markdown, and MCP examples.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Localhost URL Is Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is http://localhost:3000 in a frontend startup checklist. It does not send data to an external host and is used only as local verification guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL points to localhost and appears inside a server startup checklist. I found no evidence of outbound requests or data transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Entropy Signals Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits occur in descriptive text and a design-patterns example, not cryptographic code. The entropy alert is explained by Japanese prose and Markdown formatting, with no binary or encoded payload found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows front matter and documentation text rather than crypto implementation. I found no encrypted blob, decoder, or executable payload.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Signals Are Example Code
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections map to sample methods such as getCurrentUser in Java refactoring examples. They do not inspect the host system or collect environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are application-level sample code inside a duplication-removal example. I found no system commands such as uname, whoami, hostname, or network interface inspection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Run Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell commands for branch checks, tests, development servers, and rollback. These commands are common for code work but should be reviewed before running in untrusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command blocks are explicit and benign in stated purpose, but project-local scripts can execute arbitrary code. This supports a medium operational risk rating.

content.value_statement: Large refactors can break working behavior and make review difficult. This skill guides small, tested improvements with planning, verification, and rollback steps.
actual_capabilities: Plans refactors with target files, problems, proposed changes, risks, and mitigation steps.
Applies DRY cleanup, method extraction, component separation, and simpler control flow patterns.
Guides Java backend refactoring, including authentication helper extraction and N+1 query reduction.
Guides TypeScript and React refactoring, including custom hooks and container presentation splits.
Requires test, lint, build, and startup checks before completion.
Defines rollback reporting when regression validation fails.
limitations: It is tailored to a MovieMarketer workflow with Orchestrator and Regression Guardian roles.
It does not include executable automation scripts or static analysis tools of its own.
It assumes the project has backend and frontend test commands available.
It may need adaptation for repositories that do not use Java, Gradle, TypeScript, React, or pnpm.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Duplicate Backend Logic
use_cases.description: Extract shared Java methods and reduce repeated authorization or mapping code while preserving existing behavior.

use_cases.title: Simplify React Components
use_cases.description: Separate data handling from presentation and move reusable state logic into custom hooks.

use_cases.title: Review Refactor Safety
use_cases.description: Create a reviewable refactor plan with risk notes, test coverage expectations, and rollback criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Find Safe Refactor Targets
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module for small refactor targets. Identify duplicated code, complex branches, unused code, and the safest first change.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Focused Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a refactoring plan for these files. Include the problem, proposed changes, expected benefit, regression risk, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor With Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code in small steps. Keep behavior unchanged, update tests only when needed, and run the relevant lint, test, and build checks.

prompt_templates.title: Recover From Regression
prompt_templates.prompt: A regression was found after refactoring. Analyze the likely cause, propose the smallest rollback or fix, and define a safer follow-up plan.

output_examples.input: A service has repeated current-user checks across several methods.
output_examples.output: A concise plan to extract one authenticated-user helper, update callers, add focused tests, and verify no authorization behavior changed.

output_examples.input: A React component mixes fetching state, loading behavior, errors, and rendering.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to move data state into a custom hook and keep the component focused on rendering states.

output_examples.input: A refactor caused test failures after a build check.
output_examples.output: A regression report with the failure summary, likely cause, rollback option, and a smaller retry strategy.

best_practices: Start from a branch and keep each refactor small enough to review.
Run tests before and after changes so behavior differences are visible.
Document risk, verification results, and rollback steps for every meaningful refactor.
anti_patterns: Do not mix large feature changes with refactoring work.
Do not change test intent just to make a refactor pass.
Do not refactor authentication, payment, or schema logic without extra review.

faq.question: What types of code does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Java backend code and TypeScript or React frontend code, with general refactoring practices that can transfer elsewhere.

faq.question: Does it make code changes automatically?
faq.answer: It guides an AI coding agent through file reads, edits, tests, and reports. Users should review changes before merging.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated medium?
faq.answer: The skill instructs agents to edit files and run repository commands. That is expected for refactoring but risky in untrusted repositories.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No external network behavior was confirmed. The localhost URL is documentation for checking a local frontend server.

faq.question: How does it prevent regressions?
faq.answer: It requires baseline checks, staged implementation, test execution, startup verification, and rollback reporting when regressions appear.

faq.question: Can it be used outside the MovieMarketer project?
faq.answer: Yes, but the workflow references Orchestrator and Regression Guardian roles, so teams may need to adapt those steps.
